mixed.question: What is the capital of Australia?
mixed.fact: Canberra was purpose-built as a compromise between rivals Sydney and Melbourne.

mixed.question: How many sides does a hexagon have?
mixed.fact: The word hexagon comes from Greek: 'hex' meaning six and 'gonia' meaning angle.

mixed.question: Which planet is known as the Red Planet?
mixed.fact: Mars gets its red colour from iron oxide (rust) on its surface.

mixed.question: What is the chemical symbol for gold?
mixed.fact: Au comes from the Latin word 'aurum', meaning gold.

mixed.question: Who painted the Mona Lisa?
mixed.fact: Da Vinci likely painted the Mona Lisa between 1503 and 1519.

mixed.question: What is the largest ocean on Earth?
mixed.fact: The Pacific Ocean covers more area than all of Earth's land masses combined.

mixed.question: How many bones are in the adult human body?
mixed.fact: Babies are born with around 270 bones — many fuse together as we grow.

mixed.question: In which year did the Berlin Wall fall?
mixed.fact: The Berlin Wall stood for 28 years, from 1961 to 1989.

mixed.question: What is the fastest land animal?
mixed.fact: Cheetahs can reach 70mph (112km/h) but can only sustain it for about 30 seconds.

mixed.question: What language has the most native speakers worldwide?
mixed.fact: Mandarin has around 920 million native speakers — nearly double second-place Spanish.

science.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
science.fact: Carbon is the basis of all known life and can form more compounds than any other element.

science.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
science.fact: A single large tree can absorb up to 48 pounds of CO₂ per year.

science.question: What is the speed of light in a vacuum?
science.fact: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes and 20 seconds to reach Earth.

science.question: What is the powerhouse of the cell?
science.fact: Mitochondria have their own DNA, evidence they were once free-living bacteria.

science.question: What is the most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere?
science.fact: Nitrogen makes up about 78% of Earth's atmosphere, while oxygen is only 21%.

science.question: How many chromosomes do humans normally have?
science.fact: Humans have 46 chromosomes arranged in 23 pairs — one set from each parent.

science.question: What is H₂O more commonly known as?
science.fact: Water is the only natural substance found in all three states at Earth's surface temperatures.

science.question: Which scientist developed the theory of general relativity?
science.fact: Einstein published his general theory of relativity in 1915, revolutionising our understanding of gravity.

science.question: What is the hardest natural substance on Earth?
science.fact: Diamond scores 10 on the Mohs scale — the maximum possible.

science.question: What type of waves does a microwave oven use?
science.fact: Microwave ovens were invented by accident in 1945 when a radar engineer melted a chocolate bar.

history.question: In which year did World War II end?
history.fact: WWII ended on September 2, 1945, with Japan's formal surrender aboard the USS Missouri.

history.question: Who was the first person to walk on the Moon?
history.fact: Armstrong's famous words were slightly garbled — he intended to say 'one small step for a man'.

history.question: Which ancient wonder was located in Alexandria, Egypt?
history.fact: The Lighthouse of Alexandria stood over 100 metres tall and guided ships for centuries.

history.question: In which year did the Titanic sink?
history.fact: The Titanic sank on its maiden voyage on April 15, 1912, after hitting an iceberg.

history.question: Who was the first Emperor of Rome?
history.fact: Augustus ruled Rome for 41 years, the longest reign of any Roman emperor.

history.question: The Great Wall of China was primarily built to defend against which group?
history.fact: The Great Wall spans over 13,000 miles and took hundreds of years to complete.

history.question: Which country was the first to give women the right to vote?
history.fact: New Zealand granted women the right to vote in 1893, decades ahead of most nations.

history.question: What was the name of the first artificial Earth satellite?
history.fact: Sputnik 1 was launched by the Soviet Union in 1957 and orbited Earth every 96 minutes.

history.question: How long did the Hundred Years' War last?
history.fact: The Hundred Years' War between England and France actually lasted from 1337 to 1453 — 116 years.

history.question: Who wrote 'The Communist Manifesto'?
history.fact: Marx and Engels wrote the manifesto in 1848 — it was only 23 pages long.

popculture.question: Which film won the first ever Academy Award for Best Picture?
popculture.fact: Wings (1927) won Best Picture at the very first Oscars ceremony in 1929.

popculture.question: How many Harry Potter books are in the main series?
popculture.fact: J.K. Rowling wrote the 7 main Harry Potter books over 10 years, from 1997 to 2007.

popculture.question: Which band performed at the most-watched Super Bowl halftime show?
popculture.fact: Katy Perry's 2015 halftime show drew 121 million viewers, the most in Super Bowl history.

popculture.question: In which year was the first iPhone released?
popculture.fact: Steve Jobs unveiled the first iPhone on January 9, 2007, calling it 'revolutionary'.

popculture.question: Which TV show features a chemistry teacher turned drug lord?
popculture.fact: Bryan Cranston won four Emmy Awards for his role as Walter White in Breaking Bad.

popculture.question: Who holds the record for most Grammy Awards won?
popculture.fact: Beyoncé holds the record with 32 Grammy wins — the most of any artist ever.

popculture.question: What is the highest-grossing film of all time (not adjusted for inflation)?
popculture.fact: Avatar (2009) grossed over $2.9 billion, reclaiming its record after its 2022 re-release.

popculture.question: Which game console introduced the 'Wii' motion controller?
popculture.fact: The Nintendo Wii sold over 100 million units and brought gaming to entirely new audiences.

popculture.question: In Friends, what is the name of Ross's second wife?
popculture.fact: Ross accidentally said Rachel's name at the altar during his wedding to Emily.

popculture.question: Which artist released the album 'Thriller'?
popculture.fact: Thriller remains the best-selling album of all time with an estimated 70 million copies sold.

geography.question: What is the longest river in the world?
geography.fact: The Nile stretches about 6,650 km — though some measurements put the Amazon marginally longer.

geography.question: Which country has the most natural lakes?
geography.fact: Canada has an estimated 2 million lakes, containing about 20% of the world's fresh water.

geography.question: What is the smallest country in the world?
geography.fact: Vatican City is just 0.44 square kilometres — smaller than most city parks.

geography.question: Which desert is the largest in the world?
geography.fact: Antarctica is technically a cold desert — it receives less than 200mm of precipitation per year.

geography.question: How many countries share a land border with Germany?
geography.fact: Germany borders Denmark, Netherlands, Belgium, Luxembourg, France, Switzerland, Austria, Czech Republic, and Poland.

geography.question: On which continent is the Amazon rainforest located?
geography.fact: The Amazon produces 20% of Earth's oxygen and houses 10% of all species on the planet.

geography.question: What is the capital of Canada?
geography.fact: Ottawa was chosen as Canada's capital in 1857 by Queen Victoria as a compromise city.

geography.question: Which country has the longest coastline in the world?
geography.fact: Canada's coastline stretches 202,080 km — nearly six times longer than second-place Norway.

geography.question: In which ocean is the island of Madagascar?
geography.fact: Madagascar split from Africa 165 million years ago, allowing unique evolution of its wildlife.

geography.question: What is the tallest mountain in Africa?
geography.fact: Kilimanjaro stands 5,895m and is the world's tallest free-standing mountain.

sports.question: How many players are on a standard football (soccer) team?
sports.fact: Football's 11-player format was standardised in England in the 1860s.

sports.question: Which country has won the most FIFA World Cups?
sports.fact: Brazil has won 5 World Cups (1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, 2002) — the most of any nation.

sports.question: In tennis, what is the term for winning a game without your opponent scoring?
sports.fact: The term 'love' for zero in tennis may come from the French 'l'oeuf' meaning egg (the shape of 0).

sports.question: How long is an Olympic swimming pool?
sports.fact: Olympic pools hold 2.5 million litres of water and must be kept at exactly 25-28°C.

sports.question: Which sport uses a shuttlecock?
sports.fact: A shuttlecock can travel over 200mph when struck by a professional badminton player.

sports.question: How many rings are on the Olympic flag?
sports.fact: The five Olympic rings represent the five continents of the world united by sport.

sports.question: In basketball, how many points is a regular field goal worth?
sports.fact: The three-point line was introduced to the NBA in 1979 and revolutionised the game.

sports.question: Which country invented the sport of cricket?
sports.fact: Cricket originated in England in the 16th century and was brought worldwide by the British Empire.

sports.question: How many holes are played in a standard round of golf?
sports.fact: The 18-hole standard was set by St Andrews in 1858 when they merged their first 10 holes.

sports.question: What sport does Serena Williams play?
sports.fact: Serena Williams won 23 Grand Slam singles titles — the most by any player in the Open Era.
